agent_output:
```yaml
handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement the notification preferences API for the "notification preferences"
    feature. Users can manage their marketing email opt-out via a PATCH endpoint.
    Transactional emails (password resets, security alerts) are legally required
    and must never be disableable — the API must actively enforce this constraint
    and reject attempts to do so with a structured error response.

  domain_rules:
    - rule: >
        Transactional emails cannot be disabled under any circumstances.
      testable_assertion: >
        A PATCH request setting transactional_emails_enabled=false must never
        persist the change and must return a 403 with error code
        TRANSACTIONAL_OPT_OUT_PROHIBITED.

    - rule: >
        Users may opt out of marketing emails at any time.
      testable_assertion: >
        A PATCH request setting marketing_emails_enabled=false must persist
        successfully and be reflected in subsequent reads of the preference.

  architecture_guidance:
    - decision: Endpoint is PATCH /users/{id}/notification-preferences
      implementation_note: >
        Standard partial-update semantics. Only fields present in the request
        body should be updated. marketing_emails_enabled is a boolean field.
        transactional_emails_enabled may appear in the schema for read
        purposes but must be rejected on write.

    - decision: >
        Validation of transactional_emails_enabled=false is enforced at the
        API layer, returning HTTP 403.
      implementation_note: >
        Do not treat this as a 400 (bad input) — it is a 403 (forbidden action).
        The error body must include the machine-readable code
        TRANSACTIONAL_OPT_OUT_PROHIBITED so clients can handle it
        programmatically.

  acceptance_criteria:
    - criterion: Marketing email opt-out
      given: A user with marketing emails currently enabled
      when: PATCH /users/{id}/notification-preferences with { marketing_emails_enabled: false }
      then: >
        Returns 2xx. Subsequent GET of preferences reflects marketing_emails_enabled=false.

    - criterion: Transactional email opt-out blocked
      given: Any user
      when: PATCH /users/{id}/notification-preferences with { transactional_emails_enabled: false }
      then: >
        Returns HTTP 403 with body containing error code
        TRANSACTIONAL_OPT_OUT_PROHIBITED. No preference is changed.

  terminology_alignment:
    - domain_term: "opt out of marketing emails"
      technical_term: marketing_emails_enabled=false
      definition: >
        Boolean flag on the preferences object. false means the user has
        opted out and should not receive marketing communications.

    - domain_term: "transactional emails cannot be disabled"
      technical_term: transactional_emails_enabled must never be set to false
      definition: >
        The field may exist as readable state (always true) but the API must
        reject any write that attempts to set it false. This is a legal
        requirement, not a UX preference.

  contradictions_resolved:
    - conflict: >
        Bird v1 included AC2 (user can opt out of transactional emails).
        Bird v2 removed AC2 and explicitly prohibits transactional opt-out
        following legal review.
      resolution: >
        Bird v2 is authoritative. AC2 from v1 is rescinded. Implement
        enforcement of the prohibition (AC3 from v2), not permissive opt-out.
        MJ's architecture already reflects v2 — no rework needed there.

  open_questions:
    - >
      Is transactional_emails_enabled surfaced in GET responses at all, or is
      it omitted from the read model entirely (since it is always true)?
      Recommend omitting to avoid client confusion, but confirm with Bird.
    - >
      Should the PATCH endpoint return the full updated preferences object
      or just a 204 No Content on success? Not specified by MJ.
    - >
      Are there other notification channels (SMS, push) in scope now or later?
      Schema design should account for extensibility.
```

**Note on source versioning**: Bird v1 and v2 conflicted on whether transactional opt-out is permitted. Bird v2 is treated as authoritative (it post-dates v1 and cites a legal review). The contradiction is fully resolved — Shaq should implement v2 behavior only. MJ's spec is already aligned with v2.
